Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to dedicate about four hours a day to studying, but with all seven online courses I was taking, it was hard to keep track of everything. I'm someone who learns best by listening, so I'd often play lecture videos while doing other things, hoping the information would stick. I had good intentions to be organized, but life kept getting in the way and I'd end up cramming things together.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched certain courses, then suddenly realize deadlines were approaching and try to catch up all at once. My extracurriculars also took up significant time, so I'd study whenever I could fit it in rather than having set times. It wasn't ideal, but that's just how things ended up working out for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or if I'd missed something important. I wasn't someone who checked it every single day—more like when I felt that little wave of anxiety reminding me I should probably look at what's happening in my courses.
Q4: There were a few weeks early in the semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the mornings before my extracurricular activities. It felt good when it was routine, like I was on top of things. But then I missed a few days, and it just fell apart. Once the routine broke, I never really got it back, and logging in became more reactive than proactive.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I learn so much better by hearing things explained. I'd watch them multiple times if I needed to, just letting the explanations sink in. The discussion boards were okay too—sometimes reading what other students wrote helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. Those audio-visual resources really matched how my brain works best.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and PDFs most of the time. Reading long documents on a screen just doesn't work for me—I zone out and nothing sticks. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The interactive quizzes I also avoided until absolutely necessary because they made me anxious about getting things wrong.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would actually help me understand something or prepare for an assignment. If I could listen to it or if it was clearly connected to what I'd be tested on, I was more likely to engage with it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't obviously useful, I'd push aside. Time was so limited with everything I had going on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them even if I didn't want to. Optional stuff, even if it might be helpful, always got deprioritized because there was always something required breathing down my neck. It's not that I didn't value them; I just couldn't justify the time when I was already struggling to complete what was mandatory.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I was commuting or doing chores. I also had a study group with some classmates where we'd discuss concepts over voice calls—that really helped me process things. None of that would show up in the LMS, but it was a big part of how I actually learned the material.
Q10: There were many times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded lectures while walking to my extracurricular meetings. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back, which sounds weird but it really helped me. The LMS would just show I wasn't logged in, but I was definitely studying. My learning happened a lot in spaces the system couldn't see.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd watch a video lecture once online, then download the audio version and listen to it repeatedly over the next few days. The initial view would register, but all the real learning happened afterward when I was replaying it during my daily activities. One view doesn't capture how many times I actually engaged with that content.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I had commitments with my extracurriculars that I couldn't back out of, and suddenly all my courses had things due at the same time. I felt overwhelmed and kind of froze up—I didn't know where to start, so sometimes I just didn't start at all. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I struggled to bounce back.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from activities or confused about what was even expected in an assignment, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities were the biggest issue—I was pulled in so many directions. The fatigue made it hard to focus even when I did have time to sit down with the LMS.
Q14: There were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'watching' the content, but if you asked me what was said, I couldn't tell you. The LMS would count that as engagement, but I wasn't really there mentally. It happened more often than I'd like to admit.
Q15: I postponed the written assignments a lot because they stressed me out. I knew they were important, but the anxiety of not doing them well made me avoid starting them. It's like this cycle where the more I avoided it, the more anxious I got, which made me avoid it more. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still really hard.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have periods of low activity and then sudden bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could motivate myself to actually complete things. The pressure of a deadline was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my avoidance and anxiety and get me to act.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always in helpful ways. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that course altogether. I know that's not a mature response, but with my low resilience, negative comments hit harder than they probably should. I tried to use the CBT techniques to reframe it, with mixed success.
Q18: The structure of having seven courses simultaneously was just too much for me to handle well. Each course had its own rhythm and deadlines, and keeping track of all of them was overwhelming. If the course had been structured with clearer weekly expectations or better coordination with other courses, I might have managed better. The fragmented structure made it hard to build consistent habits.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the lack of flexibility in some deadlines made things harder—life doesn't stop just because an assignment is due. Also, when instructions weren't clear, I'd waste time trying to figure out what was expected instead of actually learning. Clearer communication would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard but struggling to understand what's expected or how to complete the work properly. They might be watching videos and reading materials but not able to translate that into finished assignments. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety or other issues that make it hard to actually submit work even when they've engaged with the content.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to downloaded lectures, discussing with classmates, taking notes by hand. My exam score doesn't reflect it well, but my assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted things. Someone could definitely learn effectively outside the LMS and have it not show up in the activity logs.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually focused or just had the page open. They also miss the emotional and psychological aspects of why someone might not be engaging.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious and want to learn, not just trying to check boxes. For me, engagement feels like being mentally present and connected to what I'm studying—when the concepts click and I want to know more. It's hard to maintain that feeling consistently, though.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening and discussing with others outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my anxiety around assignments or how my low resilience affected my ability to bounce back from setbacks. It also wouldn't show that I genuinely wanted to do well—I wasn't disengaged because I didn't care. I was struggling with managing everything, and that's not something a login count can reveal.
